summary: SKCanvasView in Blazor WASM shows severe performance degradation (120fps to sub-20fps) when drawing a scaled SKImage, because Skia's raster engine must perform per-pixel resampling in WASM which is much slower than native CPU or GPU-accelerated rendering via SKGLView.

bugSignals.errorMessage: FPS drops from 120fps to sub-20fps when drawing a scaled SKImage in SKCanvasView on Blazor WASM

stepsToReproduce: Create a Blazor WASM app with SKCanvasView
Draw the image at 1:1 size (786x1090 rect) — observe 120fps
Draw the image in an 800x800 rect (slight scale) — observe ~50fps
Draw the image in a 1600x1600 rect (2x scale) — observe ~12fps
Apply canvas.Scale(2) before drawing at original size — observe ~10fps
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: macOS with Apple M2 Pro, confirmed in Chrome/Firefox/Safari, SkiaSharp 3.119.0

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The Blazor SKCanvasView rendering pipeline (raster → putImageData) has not changed since 3.119.0. The core performance characteristic — CPU raster resampling in WASM — is architectural.

analysis.summary: SKCanvasView in Blazor uses CPU raster rendering: Skia draws to a pinned byte array, then putImageData transfers the pixels to an HTML canvas element. When drawing a scaled image, Skia's raster engine must perform bilinear/bicubic interpolation for every output pixel in WASM, which is significantly slower than native CPU execution. At 1:1 scale, no resampling is needed (direct pixel copy), explaining the 120fps baseline. SKGLView avoids this entirely by using WebGL for GPU-accelerated rendering. This is an inherent limitation of CPU raster rendering in WASM, not a bug in SkiaSharp's Blazor integration code itself.
analysis.rationale: Classified as type/bug because the user reports a dramatic and measurable performance difference that makes scaled image rendering unusable in SKCanvasView on WASM, despite working well on native platforms (iOS/Android). While the root cause is architectural (CPU raster in WASM is slow for resampling), the severity is significant enough to warrant investigation — there may be optimization opportunities such as leveraging WASM SIMD for resampling, or recommending SKGLView as the primary Blazor canvas. Area is SkiaSharp.Views.Blazor since the issue is specific to the Blazor SKCanvasView rendering pipeline.

keySignals.text: 120fps down to sub 20fps when drawing just a single image
keySignals.interpretation: 6-10x performance drop from a single DrawImage call with scaling — severe degradation.

keySignals.text: This does not appear to be an issue with SKGLView
keySignals.interpretation: Confirms GPU-accelerated path handles scaling efficiently — isolates the problem to the raster pipeline.

keySignals.text: When drawing exactly in a 786x1090 Rect, we get the solid 120fps
keySignals.interpretation: 1:1 pixel copy (no resampling) is fast — the bottleneck is exclusively in Skia's image resampling code running in WASM.

keySignals.text: I've tried experimenting with different SKSamplingOptions, but none improve it
keySignals.interpretation: Even nearest-neighbor sampling doesn't help enough, suggesting the overhead is in the resampling dispatch itself or WASM memory access patterns.

keySignals.text: We are porting over logic that we run fine on iOS and Android
keySignals.interpretation: Same code performs well on native platforms — this is a WASM-specific performance gap.

codeInvestigation.finding: OnRenderFrame creates a raster SKSurface from a pinned byte array and calls PutImageData to transfer pixels to the HTML canvas. All drawing — including image resampling — is done by Skia's CPU raster engine in WASM before the pixel transfer. The putImageData call itself is efficient (direct memory view), so the bottleneck is Skia's per-pixel resampling computation.

putImageData creates a Uint8ClampedArray view directly over WASM HEAPU8 memory (no copy), constructs ImageData, and calls ctx.putImageData. This is the most efficient raster-to-canvas transfer method available. The performance issue is upstream of this transfer — in Skia's raster draw call.

CreateBitmap allocates the pixel buffer at DPI-scaled dimensions: (width*dpi, height*dpi). On a 2x DPI display, an 800x800 CSS canvas becomes 1600x1600 pixels — quadrupling the resampling work. This compounds the scaling cost for high-DPI displays.
SKGLView uses WebGL GPU rendering via GRContext, bypassing CPU raster entirely. Image scaling is handled by GPU hardware, explaining why the reporter sees no performance issue with SKGLView.

workarounds: Use SKGLView instead of SKCanvasView for GPU-accelerated image scaling (reporter confirms this works)
Pre-scale the SKImage to the exact target dimensions once and cache it, then draw at 1:1 scale
Set IgnorePixelScaling=true to render at logical (CSS) resolution instead of physical (DPI-scaled) resolution, reducing total pixel count
nextQuestions: What specific issues does the reporter experience with SKGLView that prevent them from using it?
Would WASM SIMD optimizations in Skia's raster image resampling improve performance significantly?
Does setting SKSamplingOptions to nearest-neighbor (no interpolation) provide any measurable improvement?
What is the canvas DPI factor on the reporter's M2 Pro — if 2x, the effective rendering is 4x the pixel count

resolution.hypothesis: Skia's CPU raster image resampling is computationally expensive in WASM due to per-pixel interpolation calculations. At native execution speed this is fast, but WASM runs at roughly 50-80% of native throughput with poor cache locality for large image buffers. Combined with high-DPI scaling (2x on Retina displays), the effective pixel count makes real-time resampling infeasible for the raster backend.

proposals.title: Pre-scale image to target dimensions
proposals.description: Create a pre-scaled SKImage at the desired output dimensions once, then draw it at 1:1 scale in OnPaintSurface. This moves the expensive resampling out of the render loop.

proposals.title: Switch to SKGLView
proposals.description: Use SKGLView instead of SKCanvasView for GPU-accelerated rendering. Image scaling is handled by WebGL hardware and does not suffer from CPU raster resampling overhead. The reporter confirms SKGLView handles scaled images fine, though they report unrelated issues with DrawLine performance on SKGLView.

proposals.title: Investigate WASM raster optimization
proposals.description: Profile Skia's raster image resampling path in WASM to identify whether SIMD optimizations are active and if memory access patterns can be improved. Check if the WASM build enables SkOpts SIMD specializations for image scaling.

resolution.recommendedReason: Directly addresses the bottleneck by moving resampling out of the render loop. Small effort, high confidence, and works with the existing SKCanvasView without requiring a switch to SKGLView (which the reporter has other issues with).

actionability.reason: Real performance issue with clear measurements. While the root cause is architectural (CPU raster in WASM), there may be optimization opportunities in Skia's WASM build. Workarounds exist (pre-scaling, SKGLView) but the reporter's use case warrants investigation into whether the raster path can be improved.

actions.description: Apply bug, Blazor views, WASM, and performance labels
Acknowledge the issue, provide pre-scaling workaround, and ask about SKGLView issues
